text:
February 27, 2026 – Can America win the AI race if it can't keep the lights on? In this electrifying conversation, Jim Puplava sits down with energy expert Robert Bryce and financial analyst Doomberg to unpack the explosive collision between artificial intelligence and America's power grid. From natural gas fields powering data centers to China's dominance in critical minerals, the trio explores the urgent energy constraints threatening America's technological future. They tackle nuclear power's comeback, the political risks of the 2026 midterms, and why AI may ultimately be forced completely off the grid. A must-listen for anyone tracking the intersection of energy, technology, and geopolitics. Have any feedback, breaking news, or comments about today's show? Click here to send us a message.

Follow more of our guests' work:

Website: Doomberg – Intelligent Energy Analysis

Doomberg on X: Doomberg (@DoombergT) / X

Robert Bryce: https://robertbryce.substack.com

Robert Bryce on X: Robert Bryce (@pwrhungry) / X

At Financial Sense® Wealth Management, we specialize in serving high net worth individuals to achieve their financial goals through tailored portfolio strategies and expert guidance. Reach out today at (888) 486-3939 or click here to get in touch.

Ways to follow us

Client website: Financial Sense Wealth Management

X: Financial Sense (@FinancialSense_) / X

LinkedIn: Financial Sense® Wealth Management: Overview | LinkedIn

YouTube: Financial Sense - YouTube

Apple Podcasts:Financial Sense Newshour - Podcast - Apple Podcasts

Spotify: Financial Sense(R) Newshour | Podcast on Spotify

What Doomberg and Robert Bryce say

AI data centers are abandoning the power grid because electricity demand far exceeds what the current infrastructure can reliably supply.

Natural gas is the real fuel powering AI, with massive data centers being built directly on top of major gas fields in Texas, Ohio, and Pennsylvania.

Six U.S. states have placed moratoriums on AI data centers, citing electricity costs, water use, and local community concerns.

China has built a dominant lead across critical minerals, including rare earths, copper smelting, and antimony, posing a serious national security threat to the U.S.

Nuclear energy is gaining rare bipartisan support, with Trump's four executive orders representing the most significant nuclear endorsement since Eisenhower's Atoms for Peace speech.

Small modular reactors (SMRs) face a major shakeout, with over 40 U.S. companies working on 46 different designs, far more than the market can realistically support.

The 2026 midterm elections pose a significant risk to America's energy and AI momentum, with potential investigations threatening to stall critical policy progress.

A bifurcated global commodity market is emerging, with a China price and a separate U.S. allied price developing for strategic materials like copper, nickel, and steel.

AI is transforming the white-collar workforce, with old companies at risk of being displaced by leaner startups built from the ground up around artificial intelligence.

Authentically human content is set to skyrocket in value as AI-generated slop floods the market and audiences increasingly seek genuine, human-produced work.

Transcript

Jim Puplava:

Well, it was mentioned in the State of the Union speech with the president last night. We're talking about AI data centers and the consumption of electricity. Where does this go? Will we win the race with AI against China? Let's find out. Joining me on the program is Robert Bryce, author, and Doomberg. Guys, let's begin with AI data centers. The president mentioned last night that these companies that are doing it should actually provide their own power. We're seeing it with Microsoft resurrecting Three Mile Island. And we're seeing, like Elon Musk set up a data center in Virginia with natural gas turbines. But where are we going with this? Because I'm looking at a sheet right here. Six states have moratoriums on AI data centers. New York, Vermont, Oklahoma, Maryland, Georgia, and Maine. Some have incentive stripping. Some have transparency issues like California and Pennsylvania. So how are we going to win the AI race if a lot of states now are really going against these data centers because of the amount of electricity they absorb?

Doomberg:

Yeah, so it's a great question. We've been writing about this for several years. I think the first time we broached the subject was in '24, maybe late '23. One of the things we've written about is in a piece called Irreconcilable Differences. We deduced rather early on that AI could not go on the grid. The grid just isn't built for growth. And the electricity is worth more to the data centers than residential and traditional industrial customers would be willing to pay for it. Since prices are cleared at the margin, you would have to move AI off grid. It's taken us longer to come to that realization. There's been a whole series of hybrid approaches that we've written about. For example, AI data centers setting up shop in mothballed coal facilities, which are then repowered with natural gas but connected to the grid. But they're bringing the bulk of their incremental demand with them. We see Elon Musk in Tennessee just building an illegal natural gas power plant to circumvent all EPA rules. Elon is always an exception. That proves an important rule. And most of the states that you named are kind of blue state. And I would. I was thinking of the analogy of shale. You know, Pennsylvania becomes a shale powerhouse and New York doesn't. Texas and Oklahoma becomes shale powerhouses, and California just ignores its large resources. And we might see a similar bifurcation across partisan lines here with this as well. However, last thing I'll say before turning over to Robert, one of our operating mental models is that the human endeavor is infinite compute and the US will find a way to win the AI data race and if it has to go off grid, if it has to go on military bases powered by micro nuclear reactors, the DoD is not going to lose the AI race to China. At least not if it can help it. So Robert, over to you.

Robert Bryce:

I'll agree with a lot of that, not all of that. I think that nuclear is going to play a very minor role in the AI build out. It's just not going to be able to come online in anything like the near term. It's AI is a gas story, full stop, and we, you can see that in the data in the amount of gas-fired generation that's being built. I'm keenly aware, Jim, of your point about the restrictions, and in fact I just have now published a piece on my Substack. It's called Europe to Big Tech: We Don't Want You Either. And the subhead is if you thought local resistance to big tech in the US was intense, take a look across the Atlantic. And with the help of my son Jacob, we just created this new European data center rejection database. So the long story short is now in Europe, just since the first of the year, you've seen three rejections. One near Frankfurt, one in Scotland, and the other was in Ireland, I think. But nevertheless, what we're seeing all around the world are local rejections of local communities. Now the state list that you had I'm not familiar with. But nevertheless, these are big projects and local people are rightly concerned about electricity costs, water use, property values, etc. So we're seeing this local backlash. But I think the constraints go beyond just the power availability, and I'm not saying that's not important. But I was at the Power Gen conference in San Antonio last month and talked with a very large EPC firm here in the US. A friend of mine I've known for a long time. He said for 30 years I've been out there begging to get business. He said now they're coming to me. And in January alone, this firm turned down four multibillion dollar power generation projects because they don't have the manpower. So you add the land use constraints, the grid constraints and the labor constraints on top of the availability of gas-fired turbines, transformers, switchgear, etc. All of these things are going to slow the rollout of AI. They're not going to stop it. But I agree with Doomberg that the US is very clear that this is a national security priority. So there will be workarounds and we can talk about that.

Jim Puplava:

Well, let's just talk about power constraints because, like you've written, in Germany, I think the Chancellor of Germany came out and regretted the strategic mistake they made in closing down their nuclear plants. And now they've got eco-terrorists that bombed their grid and shut down a good portion of Berlin for a while.

Robert Bryce:

Jim, we're going to have to move on. There's no explaining Germany. I don't know. You just, as I wrote in that piece, you just can't fix stupid. I mean, you just can't fix stupid. So here you have the Chancellor from the Conservative party saying, you know, this was a mistake, but have they started to reopen their nuclear plants? No. Have they turned off the Energiewende, which is a trillion dollar mistake? No. I mean, I don't know. They're like, they're like the Brits now, unfortunately. You're just, you can't help them. They're just, they're, they're driving themselves into the ditch. I hope they like it there.

Jim Puplava:

What about the blue states? And I'm thinking of my own state of California. We've, we shut down all of our coal plants. We shut down the San Onofre nuclear power plant. We've got one left, Diablo Canyon, which is slated to go offline in 2030, which would lose another 20% of the state's power. California, as you know, kind of drives a lot of the environmental policies for a lot of blue states. So it floods out not just out of California but to other states. So it seems to me my own state of California is going the route of Germany.

Doomberg:

Yeah, California is kind of a mixed bag. Of course, it is the home of Silicon Valley and it has a huge AI presence. Stanford University is kind of a hotbed of AI technology for decades. And there's a huge data center play in California. Now how that rubs up against local restrictions, power supplies and so on remains to be seen. We note with interest that Newsom seems to be moving on to his federal ambitions. And so I guess much comes down to how the next gubernatorial race goes. Last thing I would say is the state is not going to be in much of a position to be turning away big projects and big business given the flight of its billionaire class as a consequence of the proposed retroactive wealth tax that has been floating around in California. So you have this one sort of swing state of California which has a technology lineage. A lot of the thought leaders in AI still live there. But at the same time, reality is reality. It's the most expensive grid in the lower 48 for good reason. It's overloaded with intermittent renewables. It has shut down its nuclear power and off we go. So it is a fascinating thing to watch. We read about California a fair bit, just for fun. Just want to add one thing to the Germany discussion. If you like your Germany, I raise you France. We're publishing a piece on Friday about this court case that's finally going to trial in France where the NGOs are suing TotalEnergies to force it to cut its oil production by 37% and its gas production by 25% by 2030 in order to satisfy France's duty of vigilance law.

Jim Puplava:

That's insane.

Doomberg:

And so, sorry. It's, it's, and it all hearkens back to some, you know, building collapse in Bangladesh of all places in 2013, if you can believe it. But that's the hook that the NGOs in France are using to put TotalEnergies out of business. And so we'll see how that case goes. It's the subject of a piece we're publishing on Friday.

Jim Puplava:

Now it almost seems like some of these people think electricity comes out of a socket in a wall.

Robert Bryce:

If I can just build on what Doomberg said there about California. You can look at some of the maps of where data center capacity is being built and there's essentially none being built in California. And where is the overwhelming majority being built? Well, I would say it is interesting to look at a map and overlay it. And I'm publishing one of these maps in a chart I'll publish soon on a piece called AI, Gas and the Grid. And a lot of these data centers are going to be built on or near large gas fields. So look at what's happening in the Permian Basin in Texas. Now I'm from Oklahoma, I'm not here to brag on Texas, but the Permian has not just a lot of gas, it has a staggering amount of gas. So much so that at Waha, the trading platform, the trading hub near Fort Stockton, last year, many times in the year, the price of natural gas was negative at Waha. So it's a good place to be if you're consuming power there because you get paid to take the fuel by the suppliers. So OpenAI is building a big project, 1.2 gigawatts near Abilene, just outside of Abilene. And I saw that campus last December. Chevron is building 2 gigawatts of new power generation capacity in the Permian for an unnamed data center. The same is happening in Mercer, Pennsylvania, where the president and Josh Shapiro appeared at Homer City last year talking about a data center project going in near Pittsburgh. And then finally the Marcellus in Ohio, where you see a lot of activity, a lot of data centers moving into that region, not just because of large population centers nearby, but because they're sitting on top of galaxies of natural gas. So another example, or more proof, of AI being a gas story, but the data centers are going to be built where there's fuel available and they're going to be close to these big gas fields.

Doomberg:

Yeah, Robert, on that chart, I'm not sure I haven't seen it, but we published one where we overlaid the data center buildup plans with the existing natural gas pipeline network. And it's almost one to one. It's pretty amazing. The only thing I would add, Jim, before turning it back to you, is to build off of what Robert just said, this 9 gigawatt SoftBank-led natural gas new power plant construction in Ohio, leveraging presumably Utica shale gas. There's just so much natural gas in the US, to emphasize what Robert said, the Permian in particular. As I was looking this morning, by coincidence, spot natural gas at Waha is minus $3.80 per million BTU this morning. They can't give it away, quite literally.

Jim Puplava:

Wow. I want to talk about, as we talk about AI, let's talk about the minerals that are needed. Copper. I'm going to go to you, Doomberg. You just did a podcast on copper. Give us your views on where you see copper going. Because electricity uses a lot of copper. We use it in munitions. We use it in so many things.

Doomberg:

Yeah, it was a pro-tier presentation, a Doom Zoom as we call it, our monthly premium fixed piece presentation. We did a deep dive into the bull case for copper and then we deconstructed it. Our view is that in the long term copper is a commodity and the long-term real price of all commodities is lower, and copper miners are getting better at bringing copper out of the ground. This is admittedly a contrarian case. There's a lot of excitement around copper right now. We tend to fade such excitement, broadly speaking, so we're pretty skeptical. Doesn't mean that copper can't go up from here, that it won't go up from here, but that market has cleared. It has absorbed the solar build out in China. It has, you know, it never really took off during the green energy mania. It's a mature market, it's well supplied. There's a lot of copper in the world. We don't recycle nearly as much as could be recycled. The cure for high prices is high prices. All of that jazz. And so contrary to the hopes of perhaps many of our subscribers, we come down, you know, not bearish, that's too strong of a word, neutral on the future of copper. If you must own a commodity because you like to own tangible things, our view is just own gold.

Robert Bryce:

I would, I, I would, if I could just jump in. It's interesting, and you know, nobody is as contrarian as a green chicken. So can't argue on some of his points on long-term price of commodities. But if I could expand it out just a little bit because I think the copper story is important. There's no doubt, Jim. But look, antimony prices are way up. Look at other critical, or rather strategic, elements. China has built a formidable lead across the periodic table. And I've written a lot about this, really just wrote about it in 2010 in my book Power Hungry. But it's particularly in the rare earth elements where I think the Trump administration is doing the right thing in investing in some of these mines and some of the processing. But you know, it's not just about magnets, it's not just about defense when it comes to rare earths. Remember, dysprosium, europium, gadolinium, samarium are all used in nuclear reactors as well. So I think where we are now, and I think the Trump administration is responding, but when it comes to these strategic elements, the response to China's mercantilism on this front is going to take years of effort and it won't be quick, won't be cheap, won't be easy.

Jim Puplava:

And also let's talk about their monopoly in terms of smelters. I mean, even if we mine copper, we've got to send it there to get it refined.

Robert Bryce:

And effectively all the new copper smelting capacity in the world since, I think, 2000 is being built in China.

Doomberg:

There's smelting and then there's recycling. And yes, China has a very strong share in copper smelting, but it's not as dominant as it is in other critical minerals. And there's an enormous amount of copper being mined in Peru and Chile. And I, one of the things we predicted in this Doom Zoom is that whoever emerges from this election in Peru coming up will suddenly be aligned with Trump's agenda. We've seen this pattern play out over and over again in the Western Hemisphere. I think the speed to negate any bold moves by China on the copper front would be much faster than with rare earths. I think Robert and I are kind of agreeing. I think we'll run out of those other things, you know, that other stuff, before we'll run out of copper. And you know, copper's at an all-time high in price. That's great. This means there's going to be more supply coming.

Jim Puplava:

So, as we take a look at the US, we're trying to re-industrialize, so we're going to try to bring factories here. Bring factories here. We need raw materials, steel, iron ore, copper, silver, whatever it is. It takes forever to bring a mine on board in the U.S. I'm thinking of the Resolution mine in Arizona. I think Rio Tinto and BHP have been going at that for over 10 years now.

Robert Bryce:

You're right. And if you look at the projections by S&P Global, mines that are discovered now could take as much as 20 years to come online. And you know, you mentioned Resolution, it's a magnificent deposit, but the ore body is about a kilometer under the ground. So even if they start digging now, we're a long, long way from getting production from that project. So all of these are very long lead items. And you know, I've written about this many times, but you know, it was around 1991, 1992, when Deng Xiaoping on the Southern Tour famously said the Middle East has oil, they exploit their oil. We have rare earths, we're going to exploit rare earths. But it goes beyond the rare earths. It's copper, it's tungsten, it's antimony, other strategic elements. And the US has to respond with the same kind of approach that China has. But I think with all of these, it's going to require a very clear alliance with Japan, Canada and Australia in particular.

Doomberg:

The only thing I would say is I think we would be wise to partition between US-focused and non-China-focused. And I think that the Department of Defense casts a wider net beyond just the thought that the US has to do this. Basically any country not China is on the spectrum of acceptable. So when we talk about copper smelting or we talk about rare earth refining, yes, it would be great if the US did it here. It'd be great if the big giant magnesium facility in Freeport was still operational, but it's not. But it doesn't mean it has to go back to Freeport. It just needs to be somewhere in the world outside of China. I think we're going to see, in our private investing we see a fair bit of it, a wave of US government support for rejuvenating old assets or building new ones on brownfield sites in other countries where the permitting might not be so difficult. The US just happens to have military bases, and the US has a lot of military bases around the world, last I checked. So just wanted to put that out there.

Robert Bryce:

I'll agree with that. And just add one quick point. A friend of mine, Lisa Reisman, who's very savvy, she runs MetalMiner, which is a price forecasting service, and is very savvy on all of the strategic elements. Her prediction, and it rings true to me, and building on Doomberg's point here, is that what we may be headed toward is a China price for certain strategic commodities and a rest of the world price, that there will be a bifurcation in the market with the alliance that Doomberg is talking about. And it could be that the U.S. and allied price is higher than what China is offering because we have to get away from the market where China can, just as they've done in rare earths, have other players come into the market and then just crater the market with oversupply.

Doomberg:

So that's a great point. I'd like to build on something that we've been saying a lot in podcast appearances and at the conference that Robert and I were both at last week. There's no such thing as the price of commodities anymore. There's a price at a certain time in a certain location. And we've become accustomed to a global oil price. Well, there isn't a global oil price. There's Brent, there's WTI, there's Venezuelan crude, there's Arabian crude, there's Russian crude, Iranian crude. I saw this morning that our mutual friend Sir JJ was out saying that Russia had lowered its discount to Brent to $12 a barrel to move sanctioned tankers to China. We're going to see in this bifurcated world different prices for the stuff that we used to think just had a price. And copper is undoubtedly potentially one of them. Same with nickel. Same with steel. Same with iron ore. Same with coal. Same with coking coal. Pick your favorite commodity. Markets are going to become less efficient. And I think what we're going to see is a US-driven price floor for the non-China market. They might float above that price in order to induce more supply, but it won't go below a certain price if the government can help it because they want to keep these people in business. As Robert correctly indicated, the days in which China could flood the world with cheap supply because it was degrading its environment or it was underpaying its workers and then put western producers out of business, those days hopefully are behind us.

Jim Puplava:

What about the change in politics? Because we've got Trump in. He's trying to get rid of regulation, speed up the development of mining, energy, bring back companies. What happens if that changes in 2028, let's say with the Newsom presidency, and he brings California politics to climate change?

Robert Bryce:

Time to move somewhere else. Germany. I don't know. I'll defer to Doomberg on this one. I, you know, I'm not sure. Where do you go?

Doomberg:

I would say, forget beyond '28. You have to worry about '26. I mean, if the Democrats take back the House, which looks likely, and take back the Senate, which looks possible, not only will legislation grind to a halt, but there will be hearing after hearing after hearing. And to be, I think, fair about it, there's just an enormous amount of corruption going on right now. It's a very target-rich environment and there are shocking levels of corruption, the likes of which I've never seen in my decades of experience in and around D.C., and so it will literally just throw a wrench into everything. And this hearkens back to a piece we published after Trump got elected called Whiplash. The capital planning cycle of mining, of natural gas, of oil is longer than the political cycle of the U.S. It just is. This is why Darren Woods sits at the White House and calls Venezuela uninvestable to the president's face. When everyone else was smiling and going along to get along, Darren Woods told the truth to the president and caught a lot of flak for it. And so yeah, political risk is a big deal. In fact, we see it in foreign policy. I think the entire strategy of Europe vis-a-vis Ukraine is to delay until '28 because they assume, and Gavin Newsom is over in Davos feeding the flames. You know, so we'll see.

Robert Bryce:

I agree with pretty much all of that. The political risk here is enormous. And you know, I have plenty of criticisms of President Trump and we could spend the rest of the time talking about those criticisms, including some of the corruption that Doomberg just mentioned. But on the energy front, I think his instincts, and I think with Doug Burgum and Chris Wright, you know, helping point the way, his approach on energy policy has been pretty much right all along. But the challenge is to sustain those gains past even 2026. I was in Washington a couple of weeks ago. Friends of mine are very savvy. They think that the Republicans are going to lose the Senate. Now, they might, but, you know, either way, if they lose one of the two houses, look out for a wave of subpoenas and investigations into, you name it. And so a lot of this progress that I think has been very positive on the energy front and on the strategic elements front could just grind to a screeching halt because the Trump administration and Trump's allies, not all, but many of them, will face investigations and will make no headway. But this is the challenge, I think, for the US, to build on these advantages that are natural advantages on the resource front, whether it's, you know, private ownership of mineral rights, which is incredible, natural gas and oil bounty, incredible natural resource abundance. We have to build on it, but we have to have the political backing, and sustained political backing, especially on nuclear, to make it happen.

Jim Puplava:

One of the things that really strikes me today is the wide divide between the parties. If you were to go back 20, 30 years ago, you know, they would kind of get along, they would argue on something, but they would work together. Today, the divide in terms of, you take climate change, just on the opposite end of the spectrum between both parties. So when one party takes power, like when the Republicans, what we're seeing, we're seeing development of energy, you're seeing the pushing through, trying to get strategic materials, that could all change if another party gets into office.

Doomberg:

So I think what we're watching for is, can the Republicans keep the Senate long enough for the Supreme Court to radically weaken the Commerce Clause rulings that allow for the miracle of intrastate regulation and mineral rights to basically birth the energy revolution that the US has enjoyed. And so there's a world where the Commerce Clause is weakened. We wrote about this a couple of months ago. Before the Supreme Court can be stacked, which the Democrats will undoubtedly do if and when they get control of the Senate, and therefore you unleash Texas and Oklahoma, Louisiana, pick your favorite red state, to embark upon all of these projects in a way that the federal government simply is no longer empowered to intervene. So the endangerment finding, I think you're going to see the president make moves on, you know, endangered species. And then again, if the Supreme Court acts and gives back to the states the power that the framers undoubtedly envisioned they would hold as it pertains to commerce, then I think there's a good chance that much of this momentum, and I agree wholeheartedly with what Robert said on the energy side, which is what we write about, we find very little to disagree with Trump on, which is why we're so disheartened by what we see on the sort of the crypto corruption side. That exposé by the Wall Street Journal is really eye-popping for anybody who participates in the private markets. And I happen to believe it. It's not sort of fake news, legacy media nonsense, because it's basically a string of statements of fact that you can verify. So back to the main point. There is a window for much of what Trump is doing to persist beyond his administration. And it comes down to the Supreme Court weakening the federal government from its current position, restoring its proper place in our constitutional order would be a more accurate way to describe it. So then if California wants to throw itself over the German energy cliff, have fun doing that. But Texas is going to get busy, you know, exploiting the Permian gas bounty in order to build these data centers.

Robert Bryce:

Those are, those are all good points. If I could just build on what Doomberg said there, Jim. Of all of this spectrum of issues that we face on the energy side, I think the one that is strategically, I won't say most important, but very, very important long term for the US, is the nuclear front. And this gets to the question of bipartisanship. What we have seen, finally, finally, finally, damn it, finally from the Democrats, is agreeing that nuclear energy has to be part of the mix. Now, this is from a party that has been adamantly anti-nuclear for more than 40 years, close to 50 years, actually. So what we've seen that is very positive on Capitol Hill is more bipartisan support for nuclear energy. And I will say this for the Trump administration, and agree with, you know, Doomberg on all these points about the Trump crowd and what they have done. Trump's endorsement of nuclear energy and the four executive orders that they issued last summer are the most important endorsements of nuclear energy by a sitting American president since Eisenhower's Atoms for Peace speech in 1953. The challenge now, especially after '26 and after 2028, is to keep that momentum going because the US, regardless what you think about climate change or anything else, we cannot lag on nuclear technology and the nuclear build out. We have to be able to diversify our generation sources because I do fear, long term and even short term, our grid is becoming too dependent on natural gas. And the only way we can counter that increasing dependence in a reasonable way is with baseload nuclear power. And we have to get serious and stay serious.

Jim Puplava:

In your opinion, how far away are we from, let's say, modular nukes and putting those in place?

Robert Bryce:

You know, I've been saying the same thing for 15 years, Jim. I'm, I'm adamantly pro-nuclear. You know, if you're anti-CO2 and anti-nuclear, you're pro-blackout. Well, I'm anti-blackout, but SMRs have a long way to go and I'll, you know, we could talk, we could spend the rest of the show talking about SMRs. China is very savvy. We know this, right? They're very strategic in what they're doing. The Chinese are not building SMRs. They're building gigawatt-scale reactors and a lot of them. In fact, they're building about 36 gigawatts. So SMRs will have a role. But I think there's a very valid argument to have about whether we should be building gigawatt-scale reactors or SMRs. We probably need them both. But, bottom line, I'll stop with this. The US will be lucky to add 10 gigawatts of new nuclear generation capacity of all kinds by 2035. I think that would be a stretch goal to even hit that number.

Jim Puplava:

And because China can build those in five or six years, it takes us what, 12, 15 years to build one.

Robert Bryce:

Something like that.

Doomberg:

Those are political choices though, as Robert and I are in full agreement. Look, the constraint to nuclear is not technical. It's not even economic. It's political. And we should be building AP1000s by the dozen and we should be building CANDU reactors by the dozen. And look, if the French could build 60 gigawatts of nuclear 50 years ago, what excuse does the US have today? So, yeah, but you know, to Robert's point, the allure of SMRs and distributed micro-nuclear and so on. And by the way, I was sort of throwing that in on the military side as one of the ways the US would do it if it had to. This whole AI move, it wasn't my base case, Robert. My base case is that natural gas is going to power AI, just like yours. The allure of those is that it's new and it's fancy. And people can be convinced that this is something worth investing in because that's what the US does. It invents technologies and it pours a lot of money on them and off it goes. This is our big problem with fusion. Of course, we'd like to say that fusion is a fake technology that solves made-up problems. Nuclear waste isn't really a problem and neither is meltdown risk. Those are solved with modern technology. The only constraint to building out nuclear, as China is proving, as the United Arab Emirates proved, it's just politics, culture and politics. So we have to make the decision to do it. Last point. It is a bipartisan issue. I think the Biden administration should be given credit for trying to get nuclear off the ground as well. Nobody operates with the fanfare and the flair that Trump does. And in fact, I would argue that perhaps the single greatest upgrade in cabinet members in the history of the United States is going from, you know, Granholm to Chris Wright. So Trump has done that for the nuclear sector as well.

Robert Bryce:

Well, Reagan's energy secretary was a dentist, so I'll just throw that in there. So to give you an idea of how seriously the energy secretary job has been viewed historically. I'll just add one quick point on the SMR front. I've worked with my son Jacob, and proudly so. He's in Berlin, but he and I have worked together on SMR intelligence. And we have cataloged now, documented over 40 companies in the US now working on about 46 different SMR reactor designs. We don't need 40, we need three or four. And so there is a big shakeout coming in the SMR sector. A lot of these companies that have gone public will fail. Oklo, I wouldn't bet against them. They're going public later this year. We could go through those names if you like. But there's a big shakeout. A big reckoning will happen in the SMR sector.

Jim Puplava:

Well, let me throw a curveball into this conversation. We're talking about AI and data centers. Let's talk about AI and possible displacement of labor. And you know, we had globalization that got rid of a lot of blue-collar jobs as we outsourced production. Now with AI, it's hitting the white-collar space. Any comments?

Doomberg:

I've done some investing in my private life and I'm heavily involved with a small AI startup and had a front-row seat to much of what's going on in the space. Perhaps not at the true, true cutting edge, but at that interface where AI is stubbornly trying to be deployed in existing cultures and companies, which is a huge challenge for a lot of these startups. So what I'm seeing is truly revolutionary, mind-blowing, scary, total red-pill stuff, and old slow companies unable to adopt it. And a lot of them are going to get wiped out. But how are they going to get wiped out? They're going to get wiped out by an entrepreneur starting a company that does exactly what they do, but without the pre-existing bureaucracy and culture, and just taking their share. The white-collar workers will migrate from OldCo to NewCo. There'll be some transition. But I'm not in the catastrophe camp. Having used AI a fair bit, having invested in it, having embraced it without losing our human touch to the product, AI is going to make people far better. Now if we're truly at a singularity, and I sometimes worry that we are, well then who knows. And the nice thing about a singularity is you literally can't predict what goes beyond it because the laws of physics break down. And so if we're truly at a singularity then we all have to brace for impact. But history teaches us that all these tools that are very scary to the incumbent worker base, it's just a matter of embrace and retrain. And there's a lot of magical things that can be done with AI. It's truly mind-blowing at the cutting edge. So I have very mixed views. I fear that the very definition of what it means to be human is about to be perturbed. And at the same time I see that any human could be transformed into a superhuman just by using these tools. And the tools are getting better and better and better. The doubling time is shrinking. Ray Kurzweil is going to be proven to be quite prophetic. I mean, there's just, we could talk a whole podcast on AI, but broadly speaking, if you pushed me, I would fall in the optimistic camp.

Robert Bryce:

I'm still making up my mind on this or trying to discern. I've been thinking about it for, you know, months actually, and talking to, you know, the smartest people I know. I'm still on the fence. I, I, I take Doomberg's points. I think it's also interesting. I just pulled up the Citrini research piece that was published on the 22nd that actually moved markets and you saw, you know, stocks and equities losing billions of dollars because of one article published on Substack about, you know, what it says, which follows is a scenario, not a prediction. But I think, you know, what Doomberg has said there in terms of potential lost jobs is enormous. But I also, I use Grammarly, I use Perplexity. And I'm, I frankly, I'm gobsmacked by the capabilities of these tools and how quickly they have gotten better. And so, you know, I'm an old guy. No, I'm not as old as Puplava. Nobody's as old as Jim Puplava. But I'm 65 and I think, well, I'm planning to work another 10 years, but I think, you know, what do I think about my job and my role in the world in this new age of AI? I'll finish with this thought: it's just that authenticity and the humanness of the output I think is going to be more and more valued in this brave new world, because a lot of things are scary here but that human touch, the humanness. And I do a lot of public speaking. People still want to get together and I think they will want to get together more over a beer or in a conference and say what's really going on in the world. And I think for me, luckily, because I do a lot of public speaking, that's not going to go away. That can't be AI'd out of business.

Jim Puplava:

It was amazing. I had an experience. I bought a speaker and I was trying to get it to work with my phone with Wi-Fi. I couldn't get it to work so I called the company helpline. I got an AI digital assistant voice who talked to me for about a half an hour, couldn't figure it out. Finally the AI said, well, I need to get you to one of our specialists, but at least it wasn't a call center in India. And I can understand it.

Doomberg:

So I'll give you a different story with a perhaps happier outcome. You know, we just launched our sister publication Classics Read Aloud. It required a pretty high-end recording studio given how we wanted our brand ambition to be carried over to that publication. And through a happy coincidence, we just happened to be in possession of a whole bunch of old audio equipment that used to be part of a podcast studio, a professional podcast studio. So all we did was list every piece of audio equipment we had into Perplexity and said walk us step by step through how we make all this work with each other. And sure enough, it spit it out seconds later and within an hour we were recording the first reading on Classics Read Aloud. You just have to know how to prompt it, Jim, that's the issue. And I guess at your age, it's kind of challenging.

Robert Bryce:

Now we're ganging up. We're ganging up on the host here. I don't know whether this is a good long-term strategy here.

Jim Puplava:

Actually, guys, I'm, I'm really only 60, so.

Doomberg:

Yeah, just, just some hard miles, as we like to say.

Robert Bryce:

Yeah, that's right.

Jim Puplava:

So, guys, as we wrap this up, the big thing I think all of us agree on is what's coming up in the midterm elections, which could change or alter some of the things that we're talking about here in the next couple of years. Doomberg, overall, you're optimistic about AI. Robert, you're on the fence. And I, I would say that's where I am right now, is on the fence with all this. I read the Citrini piece, which is kind of scary when you take a look at the things. But then they also talk about, you know, what it's going to do from a deflationary impact. I mean, maybe we can talk about that as we close.

Doomberg:

We could probably use a little deflation given the debasement of the currency. Look, I want to amplify what Robert said, you know, to continue this mutual admiration society that we have had going here for the past 45 minutes. The value of that which is authentically human is set to skyrocket. It just is. And an accessory conclusion to that is that technologies that enable the identification of authentically human things, videos, audio, written words, that's going to be extraordinarily valuable too. I just know it in my gut. I see it in the content world. AI slop just still doesn't pass the Turing test. And I'm always surprised at who I see using AI to clearly write their stuff. Look, I'll use AI to check for typos and give some grammar suggestions, but Doomberg's pieces are written by humans, and I think our audience, A, would detect if it weren't, and B, appreciate it. And I think this is a megatrend in the future. And so I'm cautiously optimistic. And I know that if you don't embrace it, it's going to steamroll you. So my only chance at survival is to embrace it. That's kind of been our approach internally.

Jim Puplava:

Yeah, we're doing the same thing with our firm. We use it extensively. So, gentlemen, as we close, why don't you give out how people can follow you? Both of you are on Substack. But Robert, you've got videos you're putting out and Doomberg, you've got another business going. So talk about both. Begin with Doomberg.

Doomberg:

Yeah, you can find everything at doomberg.com, so newsletter.substack.com, or sorry, newsletter.doomberg.com, but doomberg.com is everything, including our Substack and our other Substack, ClassicsReadAloud.substack.com. But the easiest way, we made it simple. We went and ejected a squatter off of doomberg.com a couple years ago for far more than we would profess to admit to having paid to do that. Good investment on their part. But yeah, doomberg.com for everything. D-O-O-M-B-E-R-G.com. Jim, always great. Robert, it was fantastic to meet you in person last week and looking forward to the next time we have dinner.

Robert Bryce:

I'll finish just the, the question that Doomberg already, you know, answered. But I'll quote the late Molly Ivins. I'm optimistic to the point of idiocy. I think that, you know, I'm long the U.S. I'm a homer. I think, you know, the US still has a lot of miles to go and despite bad politicians, there's been a lot of bad leadership. But we have a lot of intrinsic advantages over the world that I think will endure. And so I'm hopeful for that. I'm easy to find. RobertBryce.substack.com. I'm on YouTube. I'm on LinkedIn and TikTok and Twitter. But Substack is where the game is and I'm all in on Substack. It's just, after being a journalist my whole career, it's just a fantastic platform and I'm publishing all my videos and short videos and such on there, so. RobertBryce.substack.com. All right, well, listen, gentlemen,

Jim Puplava:

I would like to thank you both for joining us on the program.

Doomberg:

It was great. Thanks.

Jim Puplava:

All right, take care, everyone.

text:
Approximately 95,000 solar panels totaling 50 MW at its Sparta, Tennessee facility will be produced using the company’s proprietary sustainable manufacturing process, marking another growth milestone in the clean-technology leader’s history.

DAPHNE, AL - February 24, 2026 - TERREPOWER, formerly BBB Industries, a global pure-play aftermarket leader in sustainable manufacturing, today announced a major expansion of solar module processing. Approximately 95,000 solar panels totaling 50 MW at its Sparta, Tennessee facility will be produced using the company's proprietary sustainable manufacturing process, marking another growth milestone in the clean-technology leader's history.

More Headlines

Articles

"This is our largest opportunity to date and a tremendous success story," said Alexandra Harrison, president of TERREPOWER's Industrial Strategic Business Unit. "It highlights how we responsibly restore solar modules through sustainable manufacturing, and the value we deliver to developers, operators and end users."

The solar modules, resold by a large developer after a project was canceled, are being prepared for re-entry into the market. They reflect TERREPOWER's commitment to responsible sourcing, efficient waste management and resource conservation—enabling environmental stewardship and financial savings for stakeholders while strengthening the solar industry's long term viability.

Innovating to Meet Unmet Industry Needs

TERREPOWER's operational expansion comes at a pivotal moment for the solar industry. Recent studies show that the volume of decommissioned and aging panels is accelerating faster than projected, creating an urgent need for responsible lifecycle solutions. U.S. end of life volumes have already reached 4-5 million retired panels per year and are expected to climb to 10 million panels annually by 2030, with steeper increases anticipated as repowering and extreme weather events drive earlier than expected replacement cycles.

A volatile market underscores the importance of TERREPOWER's leadership. By sustainably supplying 50 MW with modules that would have otherwise been discarded, TERREPOWER is delivering verified, reliable and sustainably manufactured panels at a moment when traceability and quality assurance are more essential than ever.

"No one else offers this level of evaluation, cleaning, testing, inspection, secure repackaging and validation," said Harrison. "The market told us clearly: we want panels that have been verified by experts. And that's exactly our specialty."

Driving Local Impact with a Global Circular Economy Model

As TERREPOWER scales its solar operations to meet demand, the Sparta facility continues to add jobs, investment and specialized training opportunities. The state-of-the-art, 200,000-square-foot sustainable manufacturing facility is now the largest of its kind in North America, with the capacity to produce 300,000 solar modules per year and the ability to scale up to more than one million solar panels annually. At full capacity, this operation is projected to divert more than 16 million pounds of material from landfills each year while powering as many as 20,000 homes.

"This project directly contributes to the local economy," said Peter Hutchings, business development manager at TERREPOWER. "We're turning panels that would otherwise become waste into reliable, market ready modules."

Solar developers worldwide face growing challenges with aging assets, stranded inventories and the need for responsible lifecycle management. Sparta's success reflects a practical, proven and scalable pathway to redeploy solar modules that still have productive life ahead.

For more information about TERREPOWER, its solar offerings and other products sold under its Ontility brand, visit www.terrepower.com and www.ontility.com.

About TERREPOWER

TERREPOWER, formerly BBB Industries, is the largest sustainable manufacturer in the world by volume. Founded in 1987 on a legacy of innovation, TERREPOWER is a global pure-play aftermarket leader specializing in providing high-quality components to the automotive and industrial markets. Based in Daphne, Alabama, TERREPOWER has a dedicated global workforce of over 10,000 employees and an extensive operational footprint throughout North America and Europe, including 19 sustainable manufacturing facilities, 14 distribution centers, and 28 brands with products sold in more than 90 countries. TERREPOWER is committed to strengthening supply chain resilience, reducing waste and advancing the circular economy. Learn more at www.terrepower.com.

Featured Product

Terrasmart - Reduce Risk and Accelerate Solar Installations

We push the limits in renewable energy, focusing on innovation to drive progress. Pioneering new solutions and ground-breaking technology, and smarter ways of working to make progress for our clients and the industry.

name: Clean Energy
title: Opportunities in Spain's Critical Minerals Sector - United States Department of State
description:
Ambassador to Spain Benjamin Leon and Spain’s Director General for Energy Policy and Mining Manuel García Hernández for an exclusive virtual event highlighting commercially actionable opportunities in Spain’s critical minerals sector on March 10, 2026.
As one of Europe’s largest economies, Spain is accelerating investment in its critical raw materials sector to strengthen industrial competitiveness and energy security.
Spain’s mining sector includes more than 2,600 operations supporting over 30,000 jobs, with annual production valued at more than $3.5 billion.
Representatives of leading project developers – Extremadura New Energies, Iberian Resources, and Cobre las Cruces – will provide detailed opportunities featuring tungsten, lithium, and copper projects.

As one of Europe’s largest economies, Spain is accelerating investment in its critical raw materials sector to strengthen industrial competitiveness and energy security. Driven by the EU Critical Raw Materials Act, strong central and regional government engagement, and rising demand from renewable energy, defense, and advanced manufacturing industries, Spain is positioning itself as a key pillar of Europe’s future minerals supply.

Current project status and regulatory timelines

Infrastructure and permitting considerations

Investment and financing opportunities tailored for U.S. partners

This Direct Line session connects you with concrete projects and outlines U.S. government tools that complement EU funding mechanisms – the ideal opportunity for serious U.S. private-sector participants interested in mining, processing, and strategic materials across Europe.

name: EPA
title: Without Weighing Costs to Public Health, EPA Rolls Back Air Pollution Standards for Coal Plants
authors: Derek Harrison, Kiley Bense, Phil McKenna
description:
Last week, when the Environmental Protection Agency finalized its repeal of tightened 2024 air pollution standards for power plants, the agency claimed the rollback would save $670 million.
The standards limited emissions of mercury and other hazardous air pollutants for coal- and oil-fired power plants across the country.
The EPA also found that the changes would not impact retail electricity prices and would not lead to coal plants’ closures.
Donate Now In a preview of this month’s action last April, the Trump administration granted 47 power plants a two-year exemption from the 2024 standards.
One of those places is Pennsylvania, home to 14 coal-fired power plants and a long legacy of pollution from the coal industry.
text:
Last week, when the Environmental Protection Agency finalized its repeal of tightened 2024 air pollution standards for power plants, the agency claimed the rollback would save $670 million.

Environmental and legal experts said that claim is just the latest example of the agency’s flawed accounting process under the Trump administration, which no longer considers the public health benefits of air pollution regulations. Instead, EPA now only considers the costs to companies.

“If you only look at one side of the ledger, it’s always going to come out one way,” said John Walke, senior attorney at the Natural Resources Defense Council and a former EPA attorney.

The standards limited emissions of mercury and other hazardous air pollutants for coal- and oil-fired power plants across the country. In its announcement, EPA said the repeal of the limits set during the Biden administration will mean “savings American families will see in the form of lower everyday living costs.”

But the rule’s expense to most companies would have been marginal, Walke said. The $670 million is spread across nearly 200 plants, and a significant portion of the cost would fall on just one outdated facility, the Colstrip Steam Electric Station in Montana, which opened in 1975.

“That would be one of the cheapest Clean Air Act rules adopted in a generation. So it really wasn’t about the cost savings,” Walke said. “Frankly, this was an ideological policy consistent with this administration’s agenda that no compliance cost is worth spending.”

Many of the plants affected by the standards were already meeting them, said Nicholas Morales, senior attorney at Earthjustice. “So by taking away the new standards, what the administration is doing is rewarding the few coal plants that had refused to clean up.”

In 2024, the Biden-era EPA estimated the tougher standards would create $300 million in health benefits and $130 million in climate benefits and would cost companies $860 million between 2028 and 2037. Exposure to the air pollutants emitted by coal and oil-fired power plants, including lead, cadmium and arsenic, can cause cancer as well as irritation to the lungs and skin and symptoms like nausea and vomiting. The EPA also found that the changes would not impact retail electricity prices and would not lead to coal plants’ closures.

EPA’s 2026 Regulatory Impact Analysis for the repeal of the Mercury and Air Toxics Standards amendments lists some of the known health consequences linked to breathing in particulate matter, from heart attacks and strokes to asthma and lung cancer. That paragraph ends with a clear statement: “The EPA did not quantify or monetize the benefits or disbenefits” of any of these health effects.

The repeal “ensures the continuation of the highly effective and robust 2012 MATS requirements,” an EPA spokesperson said in a statement to Inside Climate News, noting that the standards have protected “public health and the environment for years.” By repealing the 2024 standards, “EPA is fulfilling its core mission without compromising America’s energy or economic prosperity.”

Reviving the shrinking coal industry has been a priority in both of President Donald Trump’s terms. In the last year, he’s forced aging coal plants to keep operating, invested millions in modernizing and extending the lives of others and directed the Pentagon to spend more money on coal-generated electricity. In February, coal industry leaders presented the president with a gold trophy labeled “Undisputed Champion of Beautiful Clean Coal.”

In a statement, America’s Power CEO Michelle Bloodworth said the EPA’s rollback is “an important step toward maintaining a reliable and affordable supply of electricity and ensuring coal-based generation can continue supporting the nation’s economy and electric grid.” America’s Power is a national coal industry trade organization.

Rachel Gleason, executive director of the Pennsylvania Coal Alliance, noted that 23 states, including West Virginia and North Dakota, had challenged the 2024 amendments in court. The states argued that the Biden administration had set “impossible standards” that would “destroy the coal industry.” Gleason said the technology requirements in the update were “unnecessary and were not cost effective.”

Part of the 2024 standards were requirements for plants to update their monitoring equipment from a quarterly system to continuous. “Not only is it not a large cost for these facilities, it’s actually beneficial for the facilities themselves,” said Kevin Cromar, an associate professor of environmental medicine and population health at New York University, whose research focuses on the health impacts of air pollution and climate change. Continuous monitoring helps operators become aware of problems sooner.

This story is funded by readers like you.

Our nonprofit newsroom provides award-winning climate coverage free of charge and advertising. We rely on donations from readers like you to keep going. Please donate now to support our work.

Donate Now

In a preview of this month’s action last April, the Trump administration granted 47 power plants a two-year exemption from the 2024 standards. The administration argued that the Biden-era standards placed “severe burdens” on companies and threatened the “viability” of the coal industry. Missing from the president’s proclamation was any consideration of the health burdens that might be placed on communities living downwind of these plants.

Cromar, along with researchers at the University of Washington, set out to investigate what those burdens might be. The 2024 update represents a minor change when you look at the national picture, Cromar said, mainly because the “vast majority” of plants already meet the standards. “But the paper was able to show that in discrete locations, it’s not an insignificant amount of health impact,” he said.

The study found Trump’s two-year exemption would mean 2,500 more tons of air pollution and more premature death in certain parts of the country near plants that aren’t yet meeting the standards.

One of those places is Pennsylvania, home to 14 coal-fired power plants and a long legacy of pollution from the coal industry. Tom Schuster, director of the Sierra Club’s Pennsylvania chapter, lives in western Pennsylvania, not far from some of the state’s largest remaining coal-fired plants. “I’m just over the ridge from there. I can see the steam from the cooling towers on a cold day,” he said.

Schuster worries EPA’s decision could mean backtracking on the progress that has been made since the Mercury and Air Toxics Standards were enacted in 2012, resulting in huge reductions in mercury and hazardous air pollution emissions. In Pennsylvania, mercury remains one of the leading causes of pollution in waterways, and residents are warned to limit consumption of fish from more than 100 lakes, rivers and creeks because of mercury contamination. Mercury bioaccumulates in ecosystems—building up in the bodies of fish and shellfish—and can persist in the environment for decades.

“The gut punch of it is that most of the plants are already complying,” Schuster said. “They’ve already installed the pollution controls. They’re already complying. And by eliminating the need to demonstrate that explicitly, we’re giving them an opportunity to cut corners.”

name: EPA
title: Roberts: Whatever EPA protecting it's not environment or us | HeraldNet.com
description:
Nothing was said about rising costs resulting from climate disasters, health care, public health, labor, infrastructure or insurance.
This EPA has abandoned its core mission to protect the environment in favor of protecting the fossil fuel industry.
They describe environmental protection and climate change as a “religion,” “crusade” and “cult,” implying they are beliefs or choices.
The laws of physics and climate change are not a choice.
Eco-nomics “Eco-nomics” is a series of articles exploring issues at the intersection of climate change and economics.
text:
By Paul Roberts / For The Herald

This month the Fossil Fuel Protection Agency — formerly the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) — announced the repeal of the Endangerment Finding it made in 2009, which provided the legal basis for regulating greenhouse gases (GHGs) that endanger human health and welfare. What the Trump administration said and did not say about the repeal is instructive.

What was said: President Trump has repeatedly called climate change a “scam” and a “hoax.” In announcing the proposed repeal last July, EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin said: “We are driving a dagger through the heart of climate-change religion.” (See: “Gutting of Clean Air Act will cost us in lives, more,” The Herald, Jan. 31)

At the announcement, Trump called the repeal “the single largest deregulatory action in American History.” Added Zeldin: “This is a big deal,” the move would end an era of “heavy-handed climate policies.” He said the policies had been used to “steamroll into existence a left-wing wish list of costly climate policies, electric vehicle mandates, and other requirements that assaulted consumer choice and affordability.” Zeldin accused Democrats of launching an “ideological crusade” on climate change that had “strangled entire sectors of the United States economy.”

What was not said: They did not mention EPA’s mission to protect the environment or the Clean Air Act’s purpose to protect public health and welfare.

No mention was made of reducing pollution, or the consequences of an ever-warming world.

Nothing was said about rising costs resulting from climate disasters, health care, public health, labor, infrastructure or insurance.

The past 10 years have been the warmest on record. Climate change poses a clear threat to public health and welfare, increasing health impacts such as asthma, cardio vascular disease, cancer, premature deaths; and increasing climate disasters such as extreme heat, catastrophic wildfires, floods, storms, melting glaciers and sea level rise. These impacts and costs are increasing every year in a warming world.

Two former heads of the EPA did comment.

Christine Todd Whitman, EPA administrator under President George W. Bush and former Republican governor of New Jersey said: “It’s mind-boggling to me how an agency set up by a Republican administration, supported by bipartisanship all the way through, has now become kind of the enemy of the environment.” She adds: “That’s a scary thing because it takes a long time to get back.”

Gina McCarthy, former EPA administrator and White House climate adviser in the Biden administration called the Trump administration’s actions reckless: “This EPA would rather spend its time in court working for the fossil fuel industry than protecting us from pollution and the escalating impacts of climate change.”

The repeal of the rule is another step by the Trump administration promoting fossil fuels and isolating the U.S. from the rest of the world on climate science, clean energy and economics. They have borrowed from big tobacco’s play book, sowing doubt in place of facts.

However, the science is beyond dispute. GHGs generated from burning fossil fuels are the primary cause of global warming. The National Academy of Science, Engineering and Medicine issued a report last September reinforcing this conclusion.

Internationally, the U.S. stands alone, and not in a good way. The U.S. has withdrawn from the Paris agreement. Nearly 200 nations sent delegations to the COP30 climate conference last November. The U.S. was the only major nation not attending, joining the absences of Afghanistan, Myanmar and San Marino

Secretary of State Marco Rubio recently criticized Europe’s climate policies as “a climate cult” while promoting MAGA policies.

The U.S. and Russia are the only major powers promoting fossil fuels over renewables even though renewable energy is now more affordable. The Chinese are leading the world in developing renewable clean energy at speed and scale. The Europeans are following the same path.

This EPA has abandoned its core mission to protect the environment in favor of protecting the fossil fuel industry. They describe environmental protection and climate change as a “religion,” “crusade” and “cult,” implying they are beliefs or choices. This practice is straight from the autocrat’s playbook: open hostility to verifiable facts, endlessly repeated lies, and replacement of facts with magical thinking.

The laws of physics and climate change are not a choice. They apply regardless of one’s beliefs, religion or politics.

The administration wants to bring this issue before a more conservative U.S. Supreme Court, hoping justices will overturn Massachusetts v. EPA, the 2007 case that gave EPA the authority to regulate greenhouse gases.

The best we can hope for is the courts affirm that the EPA was created to protect the environment, the Clean Air Act was passed to reduce air pollution and the laws of physics are beyond the court’s jurisdiction.

Paul Roberts is retired and lives in Everett. His career spans five decades in infrastructure, economics and environmental policy including former Chair of the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency Board and advising Washington cities on climate change.

Eco-nomics

“Eco-nomics” is a series of articles exploring issues at the intersection of climate change and economics. Climate change (global warming) is caused by greenhouse gas emissions — carbon dioxide and methane chiefly — generated by human activities, primarily burning fossil fuels and agricultural practices. Global warming poses an existential threat to the planet. Successfully responding to this threat requires urgent actions — clear plans and actionable strategies — to rapidly reduce GHG emissions and adapt to climate-influenced events. The Eco-nomics series focuses on mitigation and adaptation strategies viewed through the twin perspectives of science and economics. Find links to the series thus far at tinyurl.com/HeraldEco-nomics.

name: EPA
title: RVO Crossroads: How EPA's Biofuel Decision Could Reshape Grain Markets This Spring
description:
In a market perched at technical resistance and staring down seasonal headwinds, timing may matter as much as the final RVO number.
We need to see that shift in demand,” Flory says.
“If things flare up with Iran, that’s going to be a game changer for crude oil,” Blohm says.
A Decision Landing at Planting The EPA’s RVO decision is expected within weeks — right as planters begin to roll.
Soybean oil demand would tighten.
text:
At Commodity Classic this week, the biofuels debate moved from Washington talking points to farm-gate math. With EPA’s proposed Renewable Volume Obligations now sitting at the White House for review, the outcome is poised to ripple through soybean oil crush margins, renewable diesel run rates and, ultimately, how many acres farmers devote to corn and soybeans this spring.

During a live taping of U.S. Farm Report, analysts Arlan Suderman of StoneX, Chip Flory of AgriTalk and Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing by Stewart-Peterson made it clear: this isn’t just about percentages on a policy sheet. It’s about whether renewable diesel plants jump from 60% to near full capacity, whether USDA’s 17-billion-pound soybean oil forecast proves tight, and whether growers need to “buy acres” before planters roll.

In a market perched at technical resistance and staring down seasonal headwinds, timing may matter as much as the final RVO number. A bold, immediate reallocation of small refinery exemptions could ignite demand and shift acreage battles overnight. A slower rollout, or even delayed clarity, could leave spring planting decisions hanging in the balance.

Five Scenarios and a Need for Certainty

“There’s plenty of talk, but we don’t know for sure,” Suderman says of the pending RVO announcement. “But we have some ideas on what it’s going to be.”

After years of small refinery exemptions (SREs) hanging over the market, EPA has now cleared exemptions dating back to 2016. That removes what Suderman described as a lingering weight on the industry. But the key question now is reallocation.

“There’s about five different scenarios that could still come out of this,” he explains. “So there is a lot of variability.”

The most widely discussed outcome would reallocate 50% of exempted volumes back to larger refiners. But Suderman noted that number could reach 75%, and some industry participants still hope for 100%.

Related Stories

USDA Under Secretary Luke Lindberg points to strategic deals and surging dairy, ethanol and corn exports driving the U.S. toward an ag trade surplus

The new tool helps corn growers get optimum seed-to-soil contact, more efficient fertilizer use and full in-cab control of guidance technology.

Secretary Rollins tells farmers this is just the beginning of a technology overhaul at the agency following years of promises and billions spent on projects with little return.

“What we expect to happen is 50%, possibly up to 75% of the small refinery exemptions would be put back in for larger refineries,” he says. “Now what we don’t know is over how many years that’ll be. Will it be over one year, two years or four years? So that makes a big difference.”

Another unresolved issue is the RIN credit for imported feedstocks.

“What we don’t know is what will they do with the 50% RIN credit for imported feedstock,” Suderman says. “There’s a lot of pressure to move that back up to 100%. It could be something in between. It could be one year it’s one thing, the other year it’s another.”

Despite the uncertainty, Suderman sees most scenarios as constructive.

“Regardless, we see most all the possible scenarios here as being positive,” he says. “The biggest thing is not what the numbers say, but just having certainty.”

And once that certainty arrives, the production response could follow quickly.

“It’s going to take 45 to 60 days, we feel like, to really get the industry going,” he says. However, with RIN values rallying, “yesterday we got RINs up high enough that we can start profitably making renewable diesel. So we may ramp it up a little bit quicker.”

Suderman expects a finalized decision from EPA by the end of March , which is a timeline even EPA has stated.

Even 50% Is a Win?

Flory has been in direct conversations with biofuel leaders at Commodity Classic, including representatives from the Renewable Fuels Association and Clean Fuels Alliance America.

“The buzz is that it’s going to be half,” Flory says. “Sometimes the buzz isn’t right… Could be up to 75%. I think there’s still hope that it is going to 100%.”

But even at 50%, he sees progress.

“In my mind, with the way the trend was going, even at 50% reallocation, I’m going to call it a win for the industry,” Flory says.

He emphasizes that the RVO ruling outweighs other ethanol policy wins.

“The RVO decision, I think, is so important,” he says. “It’s more important in my mind than E-15 getting it done.”

The reason is immediate demand potential. Biomass-based diesel refiners have been operating at sharply reduced rates.

“They were running at what, Arlan, 60% capacity?” Flory asked during the discussion. “If we all of a sudden have to ramp this back up to 90%, 95%,” Flory continues, “we’re going to use all 17 billion pounds of bean oil in the year ahead that USDA says we’re going to.”

Markets Sitting at Resistance

Blohm believes the market has already begun factoring in future biofuel demand, but she questions how aggressively policymakers will move.

“If we came out with this information and they said it’s going to be full bore, sooner than later, the market still could respond with higher values,” she says

However, she also pointed to inflation sensitivities.

“There’s also the balance of governments wanting to not have food prices go too high too quickly,” Blohm says. “Especially with this administration still trying to bring beef prices down. So I don’t know that they’re going to immediately give us all of this great news that we’re wanting.”

Instead, she expects a more gradual rollout.

“I’m on the slow roll carryout, which would be bringing that demand up, but slowly over time,” she adds.

Technically, she sees the grain complex at a critical tipping point.

“We’re right at a perch for market prices right now,” Blohm says. “Corn, beans, wheat — all near some short-term major resistance levels where we’re waiting for fresh, big new news.”

If that news comes, either from biofuels or South American weather, the move could be sharp.

“If we can get some new bullish news, either bad weather on the safrinha crop, great news regarding the biofuels, we have reasons for this marketplace to explode higher,” she says. “If we do not get good news soon, seasonals could kick in, when prices often soften into late March. The timing of this is critical. Timing is critical.”

Acreage Wild Cards: RVOs and China

The RVO decision intersects directly with planting intentions and potentially with U.S.-China trade talks expected in April. Suderman points to both as pivotal acreage drivers.

“Two big critical factors that will impact planting intentions are the RVO and trade deal with China,” he says.

If both land favorably, soybeans may need to compete for ground.

“If both of those come in favorable, we could see soybeans having to buy acres,” Suderman says.

But timing complicates the picture. USDA surveys for the March Prospective Plantings report close around mid-March.

“Most of the surveys come in front-loaded,” Suderman noted. If policy clarity arrives after surveys are returned, “we’ll be waiting until the June survey to really feel like we have a handle on the number of planted acres.”

Blohm questioned whether corn acreage will ultimately exceed early USDA projections.

“My thought would be that we’re going to plant less corn than last year,” she said. “But is it going to be 94, 95 or 96? That’s the question.”

Even at those levels, balance sheets remain comfortable without stronger demand.

“With 94, 95, 96 million acres, including trendline yield, as good as demand is, you’re going to have carryout for corn near 1.8, 1.9 or 2 billion bushels, unless we can get some of this renewable stuff happening fast,” says Blohm.

How Much Demand Is Enough?

Flory acknowledged that the corn market has already built substantial usage.

“We’re already looking at $16.4 billion in total demand. That’s a huge number,” he says.

To materially shift prices, though, he says additional growth is needed.

“Looking forward, what does it take? We need to see that shift in demand,” Flory says. “Add another 300 million bushel, 400 million bushels of demand. We can do that if we can get some of these biofuel priorities.”

Are Grains the Bargain Buy?

Beyond domestic policy, outside markets could amplify moves.

“If things flare up with Iran, that’s going to be a game changer for crude oil,” Blohm says. “Which would pull corn prices higher.”

She’s also monitoring palm oil production in Malaysia.

“They’re having too much rain right now, and that’s affecting production,” she says. “If they’ve got lower production, then maybe we see a kick up for soybean oil demand here.”

Suderman added a macro lens, noting that grains and oilseeds have shown strong historical correlation with inflation measures.

“When you look at what the funds want to own if we see a return of inflation pressures, the highest correlation over the last 10 years has been the grain and oilseeds to the CPI, followed by energy,” says Suderman.

A Decision Landing at Planting

The EPA’s RVO decision is expected within weeks — right as planters begin to roll.

If policymakers deliver aggressive reallocation and clarity, renewable diesel plants could ramp from 60% toward full capacity. Soybean oil demand would tighten. Soybeans could push to buy acres.

If the announcement disappoints, or even arrives too late, seasonal pressure could dominate the spring trade.

As Suderman put it, the issue isn’t just the final percentage.

“The biggest thing is not what the numbers say,” he says. “It’s having certainty.”

title: Michigan Demands Its Money Back After Dana Axes Auburn Hills EV Plant - Hoodline
description:
Michigan is gearing up for a payback fight after Dana Inc. abruptly shut down its battery-cooling plant in Auburn Hills and laid off roughly 200 workers.
According to Crain's Detroit Business, the Michigan Strategic Fund sent Dana a letter this month declaring the company in default under an agreement the board approved in early 2022.
Dana has acknowledged closing the Giddings Road facility in Auburn Hills and beginning layoffs in October 2025, with about 200 employees ultimately affected, according to local coverage.
How The Incentive Was Supposed To Work The Auburn Hills project was approved in 2022 as part of Michigan's broader push to lock in electric-vehicle supply chain jobs.
On the ground in Auburn Hills, the fallout is far less abstract.
text:
Michigan is gearing up for a payback fight after Dana Inc. abruptly shut down its battery-cooling plant in Auburn Hills and laid off roughly 200 workers. State officials say the auto supplier is in default on a performance-based incentive deal and could be on the hook to repay public funds if the company does not fix the breach by April 27.

According to Crain's Detroit Business, the Michigan Strategic Fund sent Dana a letter this month declaring the company in default under an agreement the board approved in early 2022. The notice links the default to the mass layoff and closure of the Auburn Hills operation and gives Dana until April 27 to cure the problem or face collection efforts for incentive dollars already awarded.

Dana has acknowledged closing the Giddings Road facility in Auburn Hills and beginning layoffs in October 2025, with about 200 employees ultimately affected, according to local coverage. The company told reporters the move stemmed from an unexpected and immediate reduction in customer orders driven by lower demand for electric vehicles, and said it would keep running other local plants while offering support to displaced workers. FOX 2 Detroit and ClickOnDetroit detailed the closure and its impact on those workers.

How The Incentive Was Supposed To Work

The Auburn Hills project was approved in 2022 as part of Michigan's broader push to lock in electric-vehicle supply chain jobs. Dana laid out plans for about $54.2 million in investment and roughly 200 positions, supported by a $2.5 million Michigan Business Development Program performance grant. Those figures and commitments were highlighted in the original announcement from the governor's office and still appear in public subsidy databases. Both Michigan.gov and the Good Jobs First Subsidy Tracker list the deal and its job targets, and Crain's reports the MEDC had already released partial payments tied to those benchmarks.

What The State Can Do Next

Performance-based MBDP agreements typically require companies to hit and maintain job thresholds, and they give the Michigan Strategic Fund tools to claw back money or cancel awards if those terms are not met. The state has not been shy about using those tools in other high-profile cases. Attorney General Dana Nessel has recently pursued recovery of funds from a troubled EV project, and MEDC documents show the agency tracks both incentive disbursements and repayments as part of its oversight. Michigan Capitol Confidential outlines a recent repayment push, while MSF and MEDC reports describe broader monitoring and recovery activity.

On the ground in Auburn Hills, the fallout is far less abstract. Laid-off employees told local outlets they had little warning and now face immediate financial strain. Dana said in a statement to CBS Detroit that it will "support employees through this transition" and stressed that other Auburn Hills operations remain open, yet the state's move to demand repayment is already reigniting debate over whether taxpayer incentives are really buying durable jobs.
